caveats: a: OEWS data cover all Other Textile Product Mills at four digits, not the six-digit target.
a: Employment counts are not wage weights and omit within-occupation task variation.
a: Software-AI exposure does not fully capture robotics or automated cutting and sewing.
rho: No 314910-specific adoption survey distinguishes deployed AI from ordinary CAD, ERP, or cutting automation.
rho: The estimate assumes drawings and historical quotes can be digitized and governed.
rho: Customer certification and final acceptance remain human-accountable.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged from size classes rather than an observed target list.
e: NAICS product descriptions do not reveal whether sales are repeat outsourced programs or ordinary goods transactions.
e: Custom residential awning and boat-cover shops may be too project-driven or owner-dependent to qualify.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and not specific to manufacturing or the LMM band.
s5: Plans to sell, gift, or go public are not completed qualifying control transfers.
s5: A small eligible population means a few deals materially change the realized rate.
q: No observed study measures AI-benefit pass-through in textile bag and canvas contracts.
q: Government domestic-sourcing rules protect eligibility but do not eliminate price competition.
q: Retention differs sharply between commodity bag programs and engineered custom covers or shelters.
d5: The output series is NAICS 3149 and includes products outside 314910.
d5: The 2021 level was pandemic-affected and 2025 may reflect inventory or procurement timing.
d5: Berry Amendment coverage is case-specific and only supports a portion of industry demand.
o: This estimate conditions on the quantity already surviving in d5 and does not count import displacement twice.
o: Standard bags are more automatable and directly sourceable than fitted covers, sails, awnings, and shelters.
o: No direct source measures self-service or software-only displacement in this physical fabrication niche.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers buy repeat production runs or project lots against drawings, dimensions, material, performance, and compliance requirements, with revenue commonly quoted per unit, lot, project, or fixed-price contract.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code is narrowed to repeat contract and design-to-specification fabrication because NAICS 314910 combines commodity textile bags with custom awnings, sails, tents, tarpaulins, and covers; these models differ materially in customer relationship, automation workflow, pricing, and retention.
lens.excluded_activities: Commodity or owned-brand product manufacturing sold without a recurring outsourced relationship; one-off consumer craft work; retail installation-only businesses; wholesalers; luggage and handbags classified elsewhere; captive internal plants; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market U.S. mills providing repeat outsourced design-to-specification and contract fabrication of industrial bags, awnings, sails, tarpaulins, tents, and boat, pool, truck, equipment, or other protective textile covers for external commercial, institutional, government, OEM, fleet, marine, agricultural, and industrial customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation employment for broader NAICS 3149 rather than wage-weighted task exposure for 314910. Sewing-machine operators dominate at 18,180, but the largest occupations also include 2,700 production supervisors, 2,260 sales representatives, 2,210 general and operations managers, 1,780 office clerks, and 1,590 shipping/inventory clerks. OECD finds high-skill cognitive occupations more exposed to software AI and non-routine physical work less exposed. I map quoting, drawing/specification intake, takeoffs, scheduling, purchasing, sales support, compliance documentation, order status, and some visual QA to exposure, and assign low exposure to cutting, sewing, assembly, fitting, handling, and installation. Broader 3149 may understate office and engineering content in custom canvas work or overstate it if 314910 is especially sewing-heavy.
caveats: OEWS data cover all Other Textile Product Mills at four digits, not the six-digit target.
Employment counts are not wage weights and omit within-occupation task variation.
Software-AI exposure does not fully capture robotics or automated cutting and sewing.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted 314910 occupational special tabulation and task diaries from custom tent, awning, marine-cover, and industrial-bag fabricators, including current use of automated cutting and vision systems, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Custom canvas fabrication contains a meaningful digital coordination layer because each repeat job may involve dimensions, materials, drawings, revisions, bills of material, quotes, and compliance records. Nonetheless, physical transformation and fit remain the majority of shop-floor labor, so current AI affects a minority of wage-weighted tasks.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve industrial production for broader NAICS 3149 fell from 109.6061 in 2021 to 92.8047 in 2024, then rebounded to 99.6271 in 2025. The Commerce Department also states that Berry-covered DoD procurement includes tents, tent structural components, tarpaulins, covers, and canvas products that generally must be produced domestically. I interpret the volatile but near-2017 output level as broadly stable-to-soft quantity for the target subset, with a domestic floor for defense-related products and continued cyclical exposure elsewhere. The broader 3149 series may understate or overstate 314910 depending on movements in rope, cordage, embroidery, and other products.
caveats: The output series is NAICS 3149 and includes products outside 314910.
The 2021 level was pandemic-affected and 2025 may reflect inventory or procurement timing.
Berry Amendment coverage is case-specific and only supports a portion of industry demand.
d5.change_evidence: Six-digit constant-quality shipment volumes split by bags, covers, awnings, sails, tarps, and tents, plus backlog and procurement forecasts from DLA, FEMA, OEM, fleet, marine, and agricultural buyers, would materially refine the range.
d5.rationale: Protective covers, bags, awnings, sails, tarps, and tents wear out, face weather and abrasion, and support fleets, agriculture, marine, industrial, event, emergency, and defense uses. Demand is cyclical and import-sensitive, but replacement, customization, and domestic-source requirements support persistence.

caveats: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged from size classes rather than an observed target list.
NAICS product descriptions do not reveal whether sales are repeat outsourced programs or ordinary goods transactions.
Custom residential awning and boat-cover shops may be too project-driven or owner-dependent to qualify.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm review of the 45 dataset-estimated LMM firms, coding revenue by repeat contract/custom fabrication, customer type, recurring programs, standalone control, and transferability, would directly establish eligibility.
e.rationale: The official product set spans commodity bags and highly customized covers, awnings, sails, tarpaulins, and tents. Repeat custom and contract fabrication for fleets, OEMs, institutions, and government customers can fit the outsourced-service lens, but commodity product makers, branded sellers, and consumer one-off shops do not. The qualifying share is likely meaningful but remains a minority of the frozen LMM population.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 314910 ($1,257,197,580) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 314910 ($5,171,875,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $228,601 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 314910); class <5: 607 firms, avg revenue/firm $335,707 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $51,195 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 289 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,016,024 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $154,944 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 206 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,955,320 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $298,186 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 181 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,440,337 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $982,151 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 45 firms, avg revenue/firm $31,299,911 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,773,236 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $77,158,045 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,766,602 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 45

caveats: This estimate conditions on the quantity already surviving in d5 and does not count import displacement twice.
Standard bags are more automatable and directly sourceable than fitted covers, sails, awnings, and shelters.
No direct source measures self-service or software-only displacement in this physical fabrication niche.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement interviews about insourcing and direct sourcing, along with supplier roadmaps for lights-out cutting, sewing, welding, inspection, and fulfillment, would test how much surviving demand ceases to require an external accountable operator.
o.rationale: Remaining year-five demand requires an accountable operator to interpret specifications, source compliant fabric and hardware, cut, sew or weld, assemble, inspect, document, and deliver a physical bag or canvas product. Software can compress coordination and design work but cannot independently produce and warrant the finished item. The residual loss reflects customer insourcing, direct offshore sourcing where permitted, or fully automated standardized lines.

caveats: No observed study measures AI-benefit pass-through in textile bag and canvas contracts.
Government domestic-sourcing rules protect eligibility but do not eliminate price competition.
Retention differs sharply between commodity bag programs and engineered custom covers or shelters.
q.change_evidence: Representative customer agreements and bid histories showing open-book costing, fixed-price duration, annual cost-downs, rebid frequency, change-order terms, customer concentration, and margin persistence after automation would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Fixed-price, per-unit, and project quotes let fabricators initially retain labor savings. Competitive rebids and informed OEM, fleet, and government buyers share savings over time, especially for standardized bags and tarps. Retention is stronger for fast-turn, low-volume, safety- or compliance-sensitive work where design knowledge, certification, domestic sourcing, and delivery reliability matter.

caveats: No 314910-specific adoption survey distinguishes deployed AI from ordinary CAD, ERP, or cutting automation.
The estimate assumes drawings and historical quotes can be digitized and governed.
Customer certification and final acceptance remain human-accountable.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level case studies with pre/post estimating hours, material yield, schedule adherence, rework, defect escapes, quote win rates, and avoided hires across custom canvas and industrial-sewn-products firms would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Over five years, firms can deploy AI for specification extraction, assisted quoting, drawing review, nesting and material planning, scheduling, procurement, proposal and compliance drafting, customer communication, and targeted defect detection. Legacy estimating practices, low-volume variation, physical templates, safety-critical applications, false-positive costs, and limited IT staff prevent full implementation.

s5.bridge: Gallup's fall-2024 U.S. owner survey reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while 52.3% of employer businesses had owners age 55 or older. I use the employer-business intention rate as the starting point for eligible LMM fabricators and reduce the base for completion risk, owner-dependent estimating relationships, financing, and the narrower qualifying-control definition. The adjustment may overstate completions if transfer plans lapse, or understate strategic and family transfers not yet planned.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and not specific to manufacturing or the LMM band.
Plans to sell, gift, or go public are not completed qualifying control transfers.
A small eligible population means a few deals materially change the realized rate.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year 314910 transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, SBA loan records, industry brokers, and state registries, matched to ownership age and control outcome, would replace the general-employer proxy.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership makes transfers plausible, and firms with documented patterns, equipment, trained sewing teams, certifications, and recurring contracts can transfer as going concerns. Reliance on owner knowledge and customer relationships can instead lead to closure or non-qualifying internal succession.

principal_driver: Specification-rich repeat fabrication creates AI-addressable commercial and planning work around products that still require a domestic physical operator in many applications.
principal_weakness: Sewing dominates employment, and the share of firms with truly recurring outsourced programs rather than product sales is not observed.

sources.supports: Defines the 314910 activity as textile bag and canvas products made from purchased materials, including boat, swimming-pool, and truck covers, awnings, sails, tarpaulins, and tents.
sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Form MC-31490: Textile Bag and Canvas Mills

sources.supports: Reports the largest occupations in Other Textile Product Mills: 18,180 sewing-machine operators, 3,650 assemblers/fabricators, 2,700 production supervisors, 2,260 sales representatives, 2,210 general and operations managers, 1,780 office clerks, and 1,590 shipping/inventory clerks.
sources.title: BLS OEWS Data Tables for Industry Charts, May 2024

sources.supports: Finds high-skill cognitive occupations more exposed to software AI and non-routine physical occupations less exposed, with textile pressers among the least exposed; cautions that exposure measures do not equal observed adoption.
sources.title: Artificial Intelligence and the Changing Demand for Skills in the Labour Market

sources.supports: Gallup's fall-2024 U.S. survey of 1,264 working business owners reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years and that 52.3% of employer businesses had owners age 55 or older.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Federal Reserve real-output index reported 109.6061 in 2021, 94.8138 in 2022, 95.1701 in 2023, 92.8047 in 2024, and 99.6271 in 2025.
sources.title: Industrial Production: Other Textile Product Mills (NAICS 3149)

sources.supports: States that covered DoD procurement generally must be U.S.-produced and expressly includes tents and tent structural components, tarpaulins, covers, synthetic or coated fabric, and canvas products.
sources.title: U.S. Department of Commerce: Berry Amendment Covered Items

sources.supports: Describes specification review, measurement, electronic inspection, reporting, and automated vision systems, while explaining that validation and texture or performance inspections continue to require people.
sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Quality Control Inspectors

title: Textile Bag and Canvas Mills
